original_abstract:
Early diagnosis of brain tumors is essential for improving patient survival rates, yet traditional classification often requires invasive surgical biopsies. While Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) serves as a primary non-invasive diagnostic tool, manual interpretation is subject to human fatigue and variability. This study proposes and evaluates several computational intelligence frameworks to automate the detection and classification of brain tumors. Using a dataset of 3,264 T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI images, we developed a customized 2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a convolutional auto-encoder network to distinguish between healthy brains and three tumor types: glioma, meningioma, and pituitary tumors. The 2D CNN architecture utilizes a hierarchical structure of eight convolutional layers, batch normalization, and dropout layers to optimize feature extraction. For comparison, six traditional machine learning classifiers—including Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)—were also implemented.

Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed 2D CNN achieved the highest performance, with a training accuracy of 96.47% and an area under the ROC curve (AUROC) of nearly 1.0. The convolutional auto-encoder followed closely with an accuracy of 95.63%. Among the traditional methods, KNN performed best (86% accuracy), while MLP exhibited the lowest performance. Statistical analysis (one-way ANOVA, $p < 0.05$) confirmed that the deep learning models significantly outperformed the majority of traditional machine learning techniques. Given its high accuracy, low execution latency, and reduced architectural complexity compared to the auto-encoder, the proposed 2D CNN offers a robust decision-support tool for radiologists and clinicians in the early identification of brain malignancies.
humanized_abstract:
Despite advances in treatments and overall cancer survival, brain tumors remain a major cause of cancer-related deaths, with the long-term survival of tumor patients depending heavily on early diagnosis. Current clinical standards for tumor grading in the brain generally rely on pathological examination following an open surgical biopsy. While MRI is a widely accepted diagnostic modality used in the examination of brain tumors, the process of MRI-based diagnosis of tumors has for long involved clinical judgments and intensive efforts from radiologists. In this paper, a variety of techniques within the broad realm of computational intelligence are developed and evaluated. The experiments were based on a dataset consisting of 3,264 T1 contrast enhanced MRI images, which were used to develop and evaluate different patterns of classifiers. For the separation of normal and abnormal images (glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumors), a customized 2D version of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) as well as a convolutional auto-encoder network were developed and evaluated. Additionally, six widely recognized machine learning techniques: SVM, KNN, and MLP were also developed and tested in this investigation. A 2D-CNN model is built with a hierarchical architecture consisting of eight convolutional layers. In the 2D-CNN architecture, batch normalization and dropout layers are implemented in order to ensure effective extraction of feature vectors that could guarantee higher accuracy rates.

In this research, we performed various experiments to find the best model to classify Tumour and Non-Tumour samples from the dataset provided. We achieved a training accuracy of 96.47% and an area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.99 for the 2D CNN, and 95.63% for the convolutional auto-encoder. The KNN algorithm had an accuracy of 86% while the lowest accuracy (60%) was achieved by the MLP. ANOVA (one-way) was performed with a 95% confidence level (p-value < 0.05) and it was found that the majority of the deep learning models outperform the majority of the machine learning algorithms. However, the 2D CNN achieved the highest accuracy (96.47% in training), lowest latency and also has less number of layers and parameters as compared to the auto-encoder. Therefore, the 2D CNN can serve as a very reliable decision support tool in helping radiologists and clinicians in the diagnosis of the brain cancers at the very early stage.

However, the 2D CNN achieved the highest accuracy (96.47% in training), lowest latency and also has less number of layers and parameters as compared to the auto-encoder. document.input:
Early diagnosis of brain tumors is essential for improving patient survival rates, yet traditional classification often requires invasive surgical biopsies. While Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) serves as a primary non-invasive diagnostic tool, manual interpretation is subject to human fatigue and variability. For comparison, six traditional machine learning classifiers—including Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)—were also implemented.

Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed 2D CNN achieved the highest performance, with a training accuracy of 96.47% and an area under the ROC curve (AUROC) of nearly 1.0. Given its high accuracy, low execution latency, and reduced architectural complexity compared to the auto-encoder, the proposed 2D CNN offers a robust decision-support tool for radiologists and clinicians in the early identification of brain malignancies.
